what: Spatially-resolved expression of triple negative breast cancer tumor biopsies
title: Basic functionality: Visualization, domain detection, and spatial heterogeneity
text: show utility functions spatialGE, use spatial transcriptomics data set generated platform Visium Bassiouni et al. (2023). data set includes triple negative breast cancer biopsies 22 patients, two tissue slices per patient. Visium platform allows gene expression quantitation approximately 5000 capture areas (.e., “spots”) separated 100μM. spots 55μM diameter, corresponds 1-10 cells approximately, depending cell size. Data tissue slices available Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO). purpose tutorial, use eight samples four patients. GEO repositories can accessed using following links: sample_117d sample_117e sample_120d sample_120e sample_092a sample_092b sample_094c sample_094d convenience, data also available spatialGE_Data GitHub repository. tutorial, pull data repository. title: Basic functionality: Visualization, domain detection, and spatial heterogeneity
text: Detecting tissue compartments niches important part study tissue architecture. can applying STclust, spatially-informed clustering method implemented spatialGE. STclust method uses weighted average matrices capture transcriptomic differences among cells/spots. first step STclust, top variable genes identified via Seurat’s FindVariableFeatures, transcriptomic scaled distances calculated using genes. Next, scaled euclidean distances computed spatial coordinates spots/cells. user defines weight (ws) 0 1, apply physical distances. higher weight, less biologically meaningful clustering solution , given clusters reflect physical distances spots/cells less information transcriptomic profiles used. many tests, found weights 0.025 - 0.25 enough capture tissue heterogeneity. default, STclust uses dynamic tree cuts (Langfelder et al. 2007) define number clusters. users can also test series k values (ks). detailed description method, please refer paper describing spatialGE STclust (Ospina et al. 2022). ’ll try several weights see ’s effect cluster assignments: Results clustering can plotted via STplot function: can see w=0 w=0.05, can detect two tissue niches. w=0.025, gain higher resolution one clusters split third (‘yellow’) cluster appears, potentially indicating different transcriptional profile present . w=0.2, clusters seem compact, indicating weight spatial information probably high. used dynamic tree cuts (dtc) automatically select number clusters, resulting coarse resolution tissue niches, however, users can define range k evaluated, allowing detection tissue compartments.
code: tnbc <- STclust(tnbc,                  ks='dtc',                  ws=c(0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.2), cores=2) cluster_p <- STplot(tnbc,                      samples='sample_094c',                      ws=c(0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.2),                     color_pal='highcontrast') ggpubr::ggarrange(plotlist=cluster_p, nrow=2, ncol=2, legend='right')

what: Association between spatial heterogeneity and sample-level variables
title: Basic functionality: Visualization, domain detection, and spatial heterogeneity
text: explore relationship clinical (sample-level) variable interest level gene expression spatial uniformity within sample, can use SThet() function: SThet function calculates Moran’s statsitic (Geary’s C) measure level spatial heterogeneity expression genes ( NDRG1, IGKC). estimates can compared across samples using function compare_SThet() calculation spatial statistics SThet multi-sample comparison compare_SThet provides easy way identify samples genes exhibiting spatial patterns. previous figure shows expression NDRG1 spatially uniform (lower Moran’s ) across tissues samples patients 2 8 compared patents 9 14. samples higher spatial uniformity expression NDRG1 also tended higher overall survival. Trends less clear IGKC, however, looks like samples expression IGKC spatially aggregated “pockets” (higher Moran’s ) tended lower survival (note patient 9 follow trend). studies using spatial transcriptomics become larger, samples provide insightful patterns association gene expression spatial distribution non-spatial traits associated tissues. computed statistics stored STlist additional analysis/plotting user may want complete. statistics value can accessed data frame using get_gene_meta function: See table simplistic interpretation spatial autocorrelation statistics calculated spatialGE: better understand Moran’s Geary’s C statistics quantify spatial heterogeneity, tissue can simulated using scSpatialSIM spatstat packages. Also tidyverse janitor data manipulation. sc.SpatialSIM package uses spatial point processes simulate locations spots/cells within tissue. facilitate interpretability Moran’s Geary’s C statistics, first tissue simulated, gene expression values simulated. first step create spatstat observation window: Next, sc.SpatialSIM used generate spatial point process (positions spots) within observation window. , assignments spots tissue domains simulated visualized: Now, simulated tissue domain assignments extracted SpatSimObj object. Gene counts simulated way : Expression “gene_1” exclusive “Tissue_1”. spot assigned “Tissue 1”, expression “gene_1” 1 (high expression). assigned “Tissue 2”, expression “gene_1” 0.1 (low expression). Given “Tissue 1” spots aggregated towards center tissue, expected Moran’s > 0 Geary’s C < 1 “gene_1”. Spots high expression “gene_2” (expression = 1) equally separated spots low expression (expression = 0.1). pattern highly unlikely biological tissue. expected pattern yields Moran’s < 0 Geary’s > 1. Expression “gene_3” results randomly assigning expression “gene_2” across entire tissue. pattern result Moran’s closer 0 Geary’s C closer 1. visualize simulated expression run SThet, STlist created: expected, simulated expression “gene_1” aggregated (.e., clustered, red spots concentrated towards center tissue). result intently obtained setting simulation parameters xmin=2, xmax=3, ymin=2, ymax=3 sc.SpatialSIM. results metrics expected: “gene_1” shows aggregation/clustering, indicative “hot-spot” expression. “gene_2” “gene_3” show uniform random expression respectively. Notice none values far “random” expectation (Moran’s = 0 Geary’s C = 1). One reasons result effect library-size normalization. addition, obtaining extreme values C require extreme spatial patterns, unlikely observed even way data simulated .
code: tnbc <- SThet(tnbc,                genes=c('NDRG1', 'IGKC'),               method='moran', cores=2) p <- compare_SThet(tnbc,                     samplemeta='overall_survival_days',                     color_by='patient_id',                    gene=c('NDRG1', 'IGKC'),                     color_pal='muted',                    ptsize=3)  p get_gene_meta(tnbc, sthet_only=TRUE) library('rpart') library('spatstat') library('scSpatialSIM') library('tidyverse') library('janitor') wdw <- owin(xrange=c(0, 3), yrange=c(0, 3)) sim_visium <- CreateSimulationObject(sims=1, cell_types=1, window=wdw) # Generate point process # Then, simulate tissue compartments set.seed(12345) sim_visium <- GenerateSpatialPattern(sim_visium, gridded=TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) %>%    GenerateTissue(k=1, xmin=1, xmax=2, ymin=1, ymax=2, sdmin=1, sdmax=2, overwrite=TRUE)  PlotSimulation(sim_visium, which=1, what="tissue points") +    scale_shape_manual(values=c(19, 1)) # Extract tissue assignments from the `SpatSimObj` object # Simulate expression of 'gene_1' sim_visium_df <- sim_visium@`Spatial Files`[[1]] %>%   clean_names() %>%    mutate(gene_1=case_when(tissue_assignment == 'Tissue 1' ~ 1, TRUE ~ 0.1))  # Generate expression patter of "gene_2" for(i in 1:nrow(sim_visium_df)){   if(i%%2 == 0){     sim_visium_df[i, 'gene_2'] = 1   } else{     sim_visium_df[i, 'gene_2'] = 0.1   } }  # Generate expression of "gene_3" # Set seed for resproducibility set.seed(12345) sim_visium_df[['gene_3']] <- sample(sim_visium_df[['gene_2']]) # Extract simulated expression data sim_expr <- sim_visium_df %>%    add_column(libname=paste0('spot', seq(1:nrow(.)))) %>%   select(c('libname', 'gene_1', 'gene_2', 'gene_3')) %>%   column_to_rownames('libname') %>% t() %>%    as.data.frame() %>% rownames_to_column('genename')  # Extract simulated spot locations sim_xy <- sim_visium_df %>%    add_column(libname=paste0('spot', seq(1:nrow(.)))) %>%   select(c('libname', 'y', 'x'))  # The `STlist` function can take a list of data frames simulated <- STlist(rnacounts=list(sim_visium=sim_expr),                      spotcoords=list(sim_visium=sim_xy), cores=2)  # Plot expression ps <- STplot(simulated, genes=c('gene_1', 'gene_2', 'gene_3'), data_type='raw', color_pal='sunset', ptsize=1) ggpubr::ggarrange(plotlist=ps, ncol=3) # The `SThet` function requires normalized data simulated <- transform_data(simulated, cores=2)  # Run `SThet` simulated <- SThet(simulated, genes=c('gene_1', 'gene_2', 'gene_3'), method=c('moran', 'geary'))  # Extract results get_gene_meta(simulated, sthet_only=TRUE)

title: spatialGE: Spatial Genomics Analysis
text: R package visualization analysis spatially-resolved transcriptomics data, generated 10X Visium. spatialGE package features data object (STlist: Spatial Transctiptomics List) store data results multiple tissue sections, well associated analytical methods : Visualization: STplot, gene_interpolation, STplot_interpolation explore gene expression spatial context. Spatial autocorrelation: SThet, compare_SThet assess level spatial uniformity gene expression calculating Moran’s /Geary’s C qualitatively explore correlations sample-level metadata (.e., tissue type, therapy, disease status). Tissue domain/niche detection: STclust perform spatially-informed hierarchical clustering prediction tissue domains samples. Gene set spatial enrichment: STenrich detect gene sets indications spatial patterns (.e., non-spatially uniform gene set expression). Gene expression spatial gradients: STgradient detect genes evidence variation expression respect tissue domain. Spatially-informed differential expression: STdiff test differentially expressed genes using mixed models spatial covariance structures account spatial dependency among spots/cells. also supports non-spatial tests (Wilcoxon’s T-test). methods initial spatialGE release, technical details, utility presented publication: https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/btac145. details recently developed methods STenrich, STgradient, STdiff please refer spatialGE documentation.

title: spatialGE 2.0.0
text: Major refactoring STclust modular architecture. Replaced base R hclust() fastcluster::hclust() 2-5x faster hierarchical clustering. Expected 20-35% speedup STclust runtime (dynamicTreeCut accounts ~45.5% runtime). New modular functions: STclust_select_genes(), STclust_calculate_distances(), STclust_weight_distances(), STclust_hierarchical(). Core functions exported flexibility: calculate_dist_matrices(), calculate_weighted_dist(), get_hier_clusters_dtc(), get_hier_clusters_ks().

text: CRAN release: 2025-06-04

title: spatialGE 1.2.1
text: CRAN release: 2025-05-26 Added C++ code speed STenrich computations (Thank , Dr. Soupir!). STenrich function can now calculate gene set enrichment scores (via GSVA) addition gene set average expression. STenrich function can now test gene set “hot-spots” within specific tissue domain (annot domain arguments). Spatial distances can now log-transformed STgradient (log argument). Example data sets now spatialGE_Data GitHub repository. New functions spatial_metadata tissue_names quickly access names spot/cell annotations sample names. Re-assessed package dependencies. Use DelayedArray calculations. Several bug fixes.
